---
title: "02_alpha_diversity"
author: "Zulema Carracedo Lorenzo"
date: "2023-10-19"
output: html_document
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE)
```

## R Markdown

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
#summary(cars)
```

## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE}
#plot(pressure)
```

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.

#Load packages
```{r}
library(phyloseq)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(vegan)
library(tibble)
library(car) #for Levene's test
library(multcomp)
library(FSA) #for dunn test

#for exporting tables 
library(flextable)
library(officer)

```

#2.0.Load phyloseq object

#For alpha diversity we need to use a phyloseq object that has been only softly filtered. fr that reason, I used the phyloseq object obtain the 01_preprocessing script, which has only been clean from non-bacterial dna, chloroplast and mitochondria and short reads. 
```{r}
load("./results/phyloseq/ps_filtered.RData") 

#We will only use the samples from the bulk soil

ps_filtered_bulk <-  subset_samples(ps_filtered, Sample_type %in% "bulk_soil")
ps_filtered_bulk <- prune_taxa(taxa_sums(ps_filtered_bulk) >0, ps_filtered_bulk)

rm(ps_filtered)
```


# 2.1.Basic alpha diversity analysis


Alpha diversity will show you the quantifiable diversity within a sample - this way you compare which treatment is more or less diverse or species-rich than the other.

Alpha diversity requires counts from samples with equal samples sizes. This means we cannot use the CSS transformation from the metagenomeseq package, we will use unfiltered data. 


## 2.1.1. simple Alpha diversity plots (richness plots)

http://127.0.0.1:12509/graphics/plot_zoom_png?width=3440&height=1377
```{r results="hide"}
# as noted above, we will use unfiltered data

# This is a very simple plot, but will serve for a visualization

# Richness refers to the total number of species in a community (sample). The simplest richness index is the number of observed species (observed richness). Assuming limited sampling from the community, however, this may underestimate the true species richness.

#In this first plot we are representing different alpha diversity measures per plant species. The colors indicate the lineage the plant species belong to.

richness_plot <- plot_richness(ps_filtered, x = "treatment", color="water_regime", shape="Sample_type", title="Alpha diversity")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 15)) + 
          geom_point(size=10) +
          labs(color = "Sample_type")+
          #scale_colour_manual(values=cbPalette) +
          ylab("Alpha Diversity Measure") +
          xlab("Treatment")

ggsave("richness_plot.png", path = "./results/plots/alpha_diversity",
       width = 30, height = 15, units = "in")


rhizo_richness_plot <- plot_richness(ps_rhizo, x = "treatment", color="water_regime", title="Alpha diversity - Rhizosphere")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 15)) + 
          geom_point(size=10) +
          labs(color = "Sample_type")+
          #scale_colour_manual(values=cbPalette) +
          ylab("Alpha Diversity Measure") +
          xlab("Treatment")

ggsave("rhizo_richness_plot.png", path = "./results/plots/alpha_diversity",
       width = 30, height = 15, units = "in")

bulk_richness_plot <- plot_richness(ps_bulk, x = "treatment", color="water_regime", title="Alpha diversity - Bulk soil")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 15)) + 
           geom_point(size=10) +
          labs(color = "Sample_type")+
          #scale_colour_manual(values=cbPalette) +
          ylab("Alpha Diversity Measure") +
          xlab("Treatment")

ggsave("bulk_richness_plot.png", path = "./results/plots/alpha_diversity",
       width = 30, height = 15, units = "in")


#Let's make some plots only with shannon diversity for simplicity

#**The Shannon index** The Shannon index is a way to estimate both the number of different species in a community and how evenly they are distributed. It puts more emphasis on species richness, which means the number of different species present. When both the number of species and their evenness increase, the Shannon index also increases. This means that if you observe a greater variety of species and their abundances are more evenly balanced, there is a higher level of uncertainty or unpredictability regarding which species you would encounter next if you were to examine another sample from the same group.

#For example, if there are several species in a community but one species dominates in terms of abundance, the Shannon index will be lower compared to another sample with the same number of species but a more even distribution among them. In this case, the latter sample is considered more diverse according to the Shannon index.

shannon_plot <- plot_richness(ps_filtered, x = "treatment", measures=c("Shannon"), color="water_regime", shape="Sample_type", title="Alpha diversity")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 20)) + 
          geom_point(size=10) +
          labs(color = "Sample_type")+
          #scale_colour_manual(values=cbPalette) +
          ylab("Alpha Diversity Measure") +
          xlab("Treatment")

plot_richness(ps_filtered, x = "treatment", measures=c("Shannon"), color="water_regime", shape="Sample_type", title="Alpha diversity")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 20)) + 
          geom_point(size=10) +
          labs(color = "Sample_type")+
          #scale_colour_manual(values=cbPalette) +
          ylab("Alpha Diversity Measure") +
          xlab("Treatment") +
          facet_wrap(~Sample_type)

ggsave("shannon_plot.png", path = "./results/plots/alpha_diversity",
       width = 30, height = 15, units = "in")

rhizo_shannon_plot <- plot_richness(ps_rhizo, x = "treatment", measures=c("Shannon"),color="water_regime", title="Alpha diversity - Rhizosphere")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 20)) + 
          geom_point(size=10) +
          labs(color = "Sample_type")+
          #scale_colour_manual(values=cbPalette) +
          ylab("Alpha Diversity Measure") +
          xlab("Treatment")

ggsave("rhizo_shannon_plot.png", path = "./results/plots/alpha_diversity",
       width = 30, height = 15, units = "in")

bulk_shannon_plot <- plot_richness(ps_bulk, x = "treatment", measures=c("Shannon"),color="water_regime", title="Alpha diversity - Bulk soil")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size =20)) + 
           geom_point(size=10) +
          labs(color = "Sample_type")+
          #scale_colour_manual(values=cbPalette) +
          ylab("Alpha Diversity Measure") +
          xlab("Treatment")

ggsave("bulk_shannon_plot.png", path = "./results/plots/alpha_diversity",
       width = 30, height = 15, units = "in")
  
  
  
  
  
  
  
  plot_richness(tree_bac_rhizo_ps, x="Sp_full_name", measures=c("Shannon"), color="Sp_Lineage_Walden", title = "16S Alpha Diversity")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 10)) +
          labs(color = "Species lineage")+
         scale_colour_manual(values=cbPalette) +
          facet_wrap(~Stress)
richness_plot_stress_bac
ggsave("richness_plots_stress_Bac_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 25, height = 10, units = "in")

richness_plot_stress_fun <- plot_richness(tree_fun_rhizo_ps, x="Sp_full_name", measures=c("Shannon"), color="Sp_Lineage_Walden", title = "ITS Alpha Diversity")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 10)) +
          labs(color = "Species lineage")+
         scale_colour_manual(values=cbPalette) +
          facet_wrap(~Stress)
richness_plot_stress_fun
ggsave("richness_plots_stress_Fun_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 25, height = 10, units = "in")


# We can have the same plot but coloured by tribe to see if we observe patterns. 

richness_plot_stress_tribe_bac <- plot_richness(tree_bac_rhizo_ps, x="Sp_full_name", measures=c("Shannon"), color="Sp_Tribe", title = "16S Alpha Diversity")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 10)) +
          labs(color = "Species lineage")+
         #scale_colour_manual(values=cbPalette) +
          facet_wrap(~Stress)
richness_plot_stress_tribe_bac
ggsave("richness_plots_stress_tribe_Bac_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 25, height = 10, units = "in")

richness_plot_stress_tribe_fun <- plot_richness(tree_fun_rhizo_ps, x="Sp_full_name", measures=c("Shannon"), color="Sp_Tribe", title = "ITS Alpha Diversity")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 10)) +
          labs(color = "Species lineage")+
         #scale_colour_manual(values=cbPalette) +
          facet_wrap(~Stress)
richness_plot_stress_tribe_fun
ggsave("richness_plots_stress_tribe_Fun_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 25, height = 10, units = "in")




# We can also plot each species separately to see the contrast on alpha diversity for the stress within its plant species

richness_plot_species_bac <- plot_richness(tree_bac_rhizo_ps, x="Stress", measures=c("Shannon"), color="Stress", title = "16S Alpha Diversity")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 10)) +
          labs(color = "Species lineage")+
          facet_wrap(~Sp_full_name)
richness_plot_species_bac
ggsave("richness_plots_species_Bac_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 25, height = 10, units = "in")

richness_plot_stress_tribe_fun <- plot_richness(tree_fun_rhizo_ps, x="Stress", measures=c("Shannon"), color="Stress", title = "ITS Alpha Diversity")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          theme(text = element_text(size = 10)) +
          labs(color = "Species lineage")+
          facet_wrap(~Sp_full_name)
richness_plot_stress_tribe_fun
ggsave("richness_plots_species_Fun_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 25, height = 10, units = "in")



#Does the library size have an effect on the richness? We can look for a correlation between richness and library size. We do this with all the samples (no dividing by stress)

tree_BacFun_rhizo_ps <- list(tree_bac_rhizo_ps, tree_fun_rhizo_ps) #make a list with the two phyloseq objects

names(tree_BacFun_rhizo_ps) <- c("Bac", "Fun") #rename the list objects

richness_observed_plots_library_size_l <- lapply(tree_BacFun_rhizo_ps, function (physeq_object)
          plot_richness(physeq_object, x="library_size", measures=c("Observed"), color="Sp_Lineage_Walden")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          labs(color = "Sp_Lineage_Walden")+  
          #ylab("Observed number of ASVs") +
          xlab("Library size")) 

richness_observed_plots_library_size_bac <- richness_observed_plots_library_size_l$Bac
richness_observed_plots_library_size_bac
ggsave("richness_observed_plots_library_size_bac_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 15, height = 10, units = "in")

richness_observed_plots_library_size_fun <- richness_observed_plots_library_size_l$Fun
richness_observed_plots_library_size_fun
ggsave("richness_observed_plots_library_size_fun_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 15, height = 10, units = "in")

richness_shannon_plots_library_size_l <- lapply(tree_BacFun_rhizo_ps, function (physeq_object)
          plot_richness(physeq_object, x="library_size", measures=c("Shannon"), color="Sp_Lineage_Walden")+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          labs(color = "Sp_Lineage_Walden")+  
          #ylab("Observed number of ASVs") +
          xlab("Library size")) 

richness_shannon_plots_library_size_bac <- richness_shannon_plots_library_size_l$Bac
richness_shannon_plots_library_size_bac
ggsave("richness_shannon_plots_library_size_bac_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 15, height = 10, units = "in")

richness_shannon_plots_library_size_fun <- richness_shannon_plots_library_size_l$Fun
richness_shannon_plots_library_size_fun
ggsave("richness_shannon_plots_library_size_fun_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 15, height = 10, units = "in")

#with Shannon it doesn't seem to be a correlation between library size and richness



#It seems to be a correlation between library size and number of ASVs for the 16S data
#We can add a pearson correlation index to the plot

#We can add statistics to our previous plot

pearson_observed <- lapply(tree_BacFun_rhizo_ps, function (physeq_object)
          plot_richness(physeq_object, x="library_size", measures=c("Observed"))+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          ylab("Observed number of ASVs") +
          xlab("Library size")
          + stat_cor(method = "pearson"))

pearson_observed_bac <- pearson_observed$Bac
pearson_observed_bac
ggsave("pearson_observed_bac_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 15, height = 10, units = "in")

pearson_observed_fun <- pearson_observed$Fun
pearson_observed_fun
ggsave("pearson_observed_fun_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 15, height = 10, units = "in")

#When we check observed diversity, there is a strong correlation (R=0.73) for bacteria and moderate (R=0.4) for fungi between library size and richness. 


pearson_shannon <- lapply(tree_BacFun_rhizo_ps, function (physeq_object)
          plot_richness(physeq_object, x="library_size", measures=c("Shannon"))+
          theme_bw()+
          theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 90))+
          ylab("Alpha Diversity Measure") +
          xlab("Library size")
          + stat_cor(method = "pearson"))

pearson_shannon_bac <- pearson_shannon$Bac
pearson_shannon_bac
ggsave("pearson_shannon_bac_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 15, height = 10, units = "in")

pearson_shannon_fun <- pearson_shannon$Fun
pearson_shannon_fun
ggsave("pearson_shannon_fun_Aug23.png", path = "./03_Alpha_diversity/results/Final_paper",
       width = 15, height = 10, units = "in")

#When we check shannon diversity, there is weak correlation for bacteria and fungi, R=0.2 and R=0.12 respectively, between library size and richness. 


```

#Plots
```{r}

# Calculate alpha diversity with phyloseq
alpha_div <- estimate_richness(ps_filtered_bulk, measures = c("Shannon", "Simpson", "Observed"))

# Add sample names
alpha_div$Sample <- rownames(alpha_div)
rownames(alpha_div) <- gsub("\\.", "-", rownames(alpha_div)) #fix sample names

#add metadata
metadata <- as.data.frame(as.matrix(sample_data(ps_filtered_bulk)))
metadata <- metadata[, 1:4] #keep usefull columns
str(metadata)


#join both dataframes
# Convert row names into a column
alpha_div$Sample_id <- rownames(alpha_div)
metadata$Sample_id <- rownames(metadata)

# Merge dataframes by "Sample"
alpha_div <- merge(alpha_div, metadata, by = "Sample_id", all.x = TRUE)

#order names for plot
alpha_div$treatment <- factor(alpha_div$treatment, c("Control", "WCS417", "WCS315", "CHA0"))
alpha_div$water_regime <- factor(alpha_div$water_regime, c("WW", "DR"))
                                 
# Plot Shannon diversity
ggplot(alpha_div, aes(x = treatment, y = Shannon, color = treatment)) +
         geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.8, position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +  # Dodge to separate treatments
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#648FFF", "#DC267F", "#FE6100", "#FFB000")) +
  facet_wrap(~ water_regime) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(colour = "black", size = 10, face = "plain"))+
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(colour = "black", size = 12, angle = 70, hjust = 1, face = "plain"))

mapply(function(x)
  ggsave(
  plot = last_plot(),
  filename =x,
  path = "./results/plots/alpha_diversity",
  scale = 1,
  width = 12,
  height = 12,
  units = "cm",
  dpi = 300),
x=c("shannon_March25.svg","shannon_March25.png"))
 

# Plot Simpson diversity
ggplot(alpha_div, aes(x = treatment, y = Simpson, color = treatment)) +
         geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.8, position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +  # Dodge to separate treatments
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#648FFF", "#DC267F", "#FE6100", "#FFB000")) +
  facet_wrap(~ water_regime) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(colour = "black", size = 10, face = "plain"))+
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(colour = "black", size = 12, angle = 70, hjust = 1, face = "plain"))

mapply(function(x)
  ggsave(
  plot = last_plot(),
  filename =x,
  path = "./results/plots/alpha_diversity",
  scale = 1,
  width = 12,
  height = 12,
  units = "cm",
  dpi = 300),
x=c("simpson_March25.svg","simpson_March25.png"))
 

#Plot observed

ggplot(alpha_div, aes(x = treatment, y = Observed, color = treatment)) +
         geom_point(size = 5, alpha = 0.8, position = position_dodge(width = 0.5)) +  # Dodge to separate treatments
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#648FFF", "#DC267F", "#FE6100", "#FFB000")) +
  facet_wrap(~ water_regime) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_text(colour = "black", size = 10, face = "plain"))+
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(colour = "black", size = 12, angle = 70, hjust = 1, face = "plain"))

mapply(function(x)
  ggsave(
  plot = last_plot(),
  filename =x,
  path = "./results/plots/alpha_diversity",
  scale = 1,
  width = 12,
  height = 12,
  units = "cm",
  dpi = 300),
x=c("observed_March25.svg","observed_March25.png"))


#Observed Species: Just counts how many species are present (richness).
#Shannon Index: Accounts for both richness and evenness—higher values mean greater diversity.
#Simpson Index: Focuses more on dominance, meaning if a few species dominate, diversity is low.
```

#statistics

```{r}
#I will perfomed the statisticl test separatley for WW and DR
alpha_div_WW <- filter(alpha_div, water_regime == "WW")
alpha_div_DR <- filter(alpha_div, water_regime == "DR")


#let's work first with the WW samples
#Shannon
#check for normality
shapiro.test(alpha_div_WW$Shannon)  # p > 0.05 means normal distribution

#check homogeneity of variance
leveneTest(Shannon ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_WW) # p-value > 0.05, go for anova

#anova
anova_result <- aov(Shannon ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_WW)

# Get the summary of the ANOVA result
anova_summary <- summary(anova_result)

# Extract the necessary components from the summary
anova_df <- as.data.frame(anova_summary[[1]])

# Round the values for presentation (optional)
anova_df$`Pr(>F)` <- formatC(anova_df$`Pr(>F)`, format = "f", digits = 3)
anova_df$`F value` <- round(anova_df$`F value`, 2)
anova_df$`Df` <- round(anova_df$`Df`, 2)
anova_df$`Sum Sq` <- round(anova_df$`Sum Sq`, 2)

# Create flextable for publication-ready format

anova_ft <- flextable(anova_df) |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  autofit() |>
  set_header_labels(
    Df = "DF", 
    Sum.Sq = "Sum Sq", 
    `Mean Sq` = "Mean Sq", 
    `F value` = "F-value", 
    `Pr(>F)` = "p-value"
  )

# Save table as a Word document
library(officer)
doc <- read_docx() |>
  body_add_par("Table: ANOVA Results for Shannon Diversity", style = "heading 2") |>
  body_add_flextable(anova_ft)

# Save document
print(doc, target = "ANOVA_results_Shannon_WW.docx")

#simpson
#check for normality
shapiro.test(alpha_div_WW$Simpson)  # p < 0.05 means not normal distribution

#check homogeneity of variance
leveneTest(Simpson ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_WW) # p-value > 0.05

#kruskal-wallis
kruskal <- kruskal.test(Simpson ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_WW) #p-value < 0.05

# Prepare the Kruskal-Wallis result for table
kruskal_df <- data.frame(
  Test = "Kruskal-Wallis",
  Comparison = NA,  # No comparison for the Kruskal-Wallis test
  Statistic = round(kruskal$statistic, 2),
  p_value = formatC(kruskal$p.value, format = "f", digits = 3)
)

# Create flextable for Kruskal-Wallis result

kruskal_ft <- flextable(kruskal_df) |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  autofit() |>
  set_header_labels(
    Test = "Test",
    Comparison = "Comparison",
    Statistic = "Statistic",
    p_value = "p-value"
  )

# Save Kruskal-Wallis table to Word document

doc_kruskal <- read_docx() |>
  body_add_par("Table: Kruskal-Wallis Test Results", style = "heading 2") |>
  body_add_flextable(kruskal_ft)

# Save the document
print(doc_kruskal, target = "Kruskal_Wallis_simpson_WW.docx")

#Posthoc
posthoc <- dunnTest(Simpson ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_WW, method = "bh")

# Prepare the Dunn's test results for table
posthoc_df <- as.data.frame(posthoc$res)  # extract results from Dunn's test

# Add a "Test" column to posthoc_df
posthoc_df$Test <- "Dunn's Test"

# Rename columns to match
colnames(posthoc_df) <- c("Comparison", "Z", "P.adj", "p_value", "Test")

# Create flextable for Dunn's test result
posthoc_ft <- flextable(posthoc_df) |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  autofit() |>
  set_header_labels(
    Test = "Test",
    Comparison = "Comparison",
    Z = "Z-value",
    p_value = "p-value",
    P.adj = "Adjusted p-value"
  )

# Save Dunn's test table to Word document
doc_posthoc <- read_docx() |>
  body_add_par("Table: Dunn's Post-Hoc Test Results", style = "heading 2") |>
  body_add_flextable(posthoc_ft)

# Save the document
print(doc_posthoc, target = "Dunns_Posthoc_simpson_WW.docx")


#observed
#check for normality
shapiro.test(alpha_div_WW$Observed)  # p > 0.05 means normal distribution

#check homogeneity of variance
leveneTest(Observed ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_WW) # p-value > 0.05

#anova
anova_result <- aov(Observed ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_WW)

# Get the summary of the ANOVA result
anova_summary <- summary(anova_result)

# Extract the necessary components from the summary
anova_df <- as.data.frame(anova_summary[[1]])

# Round the values for presentation (optional)
anova_df$`Pr(>F)` <- formatC(anova_df$`Pr(>F)`, format = "f", digits = 3)
anova_df$`F value` <- round(anova_df$`F value`, 2)
anova_df$`Df` <- round(anova_df$`Df`, 2)
anova_df$`Sum Sq` <- round(anova_df$`Sum Sq`, 2)

# Create flextable for publication-ready format

anova_ft <- flextable(anova_df) |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  autofit() |>
  set_header_labels(
    Df = "DF", 
    Sum.Sq = "Sum Sq", 
    `Mean Sq` = "Mean Sq", 
    `F value` = "F-value", 
    `Pr(>F)` = "p-value"
  )

# Save table as a Word document
library(officer)
doc <- read_docx() |>
  body_add_par("Table: ANOVA Results for Observed Diversity", style = "heading 2") |>
  body_add_flextable(anova_ft)

# Save document
print(doc, target = "ANOVA_results_observed_WW.docx")


#let's nor check the DR samples
#Shannon
#check for normality
shapiro.test(alpha_div_DR$Shannon)  # p > 0.05 means normal distribution

#check homogeneity of variance
leveneTest(Shannon ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_DR) # p-value > 0.05, go for anova

#anova
anova_result <- aov(Shannon ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_DR)

# Get the summary of the ANOVA result
anova_summary <- summary(anova_result)

# Extract the necessary components from the summary
anova_df <- as.data.frame(anova_summary[[1]])

# Round the values for presentation (optional)
anova_df$`Pr(>F)` <- formatC(anova_df$`Pr(>F)`, format = "f", digits = 3)
anova_df$`F value` <- round(anova_df$`F value`, 2)
anova_df$`Df` <- round(anova_df$`Df`, 2)
anova_df$`Sum Sq` <- round(anova_df$`Sum Sq`, 2)

# Create flextable for publication-ready format

anova_ft <- flextable(anova_df) |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  autofit() |>
  set_header_labels(
    Df = "DF", 
    Sum.Sq = "Sum Sq", 
    `Mean Sq` = "Mean Sq", 
    `F value` = "F-value", 
    `Pr(>F)` = "p-value"
  )

# Save table as a Word document
library(officer)
doc <- read_docx() |>
  body_add_par("Table: ANOVA Results for Shannon Diversity", style = "heading 2") |>
  body_add_flextable(anova_ft)

# Save document
print(doc, target = "ANOVA_results_Shannon_DR.docx")

#simpson
#check for normality
shapiro.test(alpha_div_DR$Simpson)  # p > 0.05 means normal distribution

#check homogeneity of variance
leveneTest(Simpson ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_DR) # p-value > 0.05

#anova
anova_result <- aov(Simpson ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_DR)

# Get the summary of the ANOVA result
anova_summary <- summary(anova_result)

# Extract the necessary components from the summary
anova_df <- as.data.frame(anova_summary[[1]])

# Round the values for presentation (optional)
anova_df$`Pr(>F)` <- formatC(anova_df$`Pr(>F)`, format = "f", digits = 3)
anova_df$`F value` <- round(anova_df$`F value`, 2)
anova_df$`Df` <- round(anova_df$`Df`, 2)
anova_df$`Sum Sq` <- round(anova_df$`Sum Sq`, 2)

# Create flextable for publication-ready format

anova_ft <- flextable(anova_df) |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  autofit() |>
  set_header_labels(
    Df = "DF", 
    Sum.Sq = "Sum Sq", 
    `Mean Sq` = "Mean Sq", 
    `F value` = "F-value", 
    `Pr(>F)` = "p-value"
  )

# Save table as a Word document
library(officer)
doc <- read_docx() |>
  body_add_par("Table: ANOVA Results for Simpson Diversity", style = "heading 2") |>
  body_add_flextable(anova_ft)

# Save document
print(doc, target = "ANOVA_results_simpson_DR.docx")


#observed
#check for normality
shapiro.test(alpha_div_DR$Observed)  # p > 0.05 means normal distribution

#check homogeneity of variance
leveneTest(Observed ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_DR) # p-value > 0.05

#anova
anova_result <- aov(Observed ~ treatment, data = alpha_div_DR)

# Get the summary of the ANOVA result
anova_summary <- summary(anova_result)

# Extract the necessary components from the summary
anova_df <- as.data.frame(anova_summary[[1]])

# Round the values for presentation (optional)
anova_df$`Pr(>F)` <- formatC(anova_df$`Pr(>F)`, format = "f", digits = 3)
anova_df$`F value` <- round(anova_df$`F value`, 2)
anova_df$`Df` <- round(anova_df$`Df`, 2)
anova_df$`Sum Sq` <- round(anova_df$`Sum Sq`, 2)

# Create flextable for publication-ready format

anova_ft <- flextable(anova_df) |>
  theme_booktabs() |>
  autofit() |>
  set_header_labels(
    Df = "DF", 
    Sum.Sq = "Sum Sq", 
    `Mean Sq` = "Mean Sq", 
    `F value` = "F-value", 
    `Pr(>F)` = "p-value"
  )

# Save table as a Word document
library(officer)
doc <- read_docx() |>
  body_add_par("Table: ANOVA Results for Observed Diversity", style = "heading 2") |>
  body_add_flextable(anova_ft)

# Save document
print(doc, target = "ANOVA_results_observed_DR.docx")



```
